name: Justice Defenders
mission: Defending justice with defenceless communities in Africa through legal education, training, and practice.
theoryOfChange: Justice Defenders has a published 'Theory of Change' as part of their 'The Justice Defenders Way' document on their website. This document outlines their approach, values, and 2025-2027 strategy. Their approach involves working inside prisons to provide legal education, training, and practice, aiming to transform prisons into places of learning and service and build a movement that reimagines justice from the inside out. They explicitly state, 'Our 2025-2027 strategy will grow our movement of defending the defenceless. Over the next three years, we will shift power within more prisons and continue to reshape justice systems from the inside out. We will expand legal education and practice, create new pathways from incarceration to advocacy, and advance relational systems change that restores trust across justice institutions.' An external case study also confirms that Justice Defenders developed a 'powerful theory of change' through workshops and facilitated sessions.

zakat.claim_evidence: There is no explicit mention of "zakat" on the provided information about Justice Defenders (USA) or its donation methods. The organization is described as a US nonprofit working in Africa, and while it accepts donations, there is no indication of a dedicated zakat fund or campaign by the US entity. (Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Justice_Defenders)

baseline_narrative.summary: Justice Defenders (USA) is a non-profit organization dedicated to defending justice within defenseless communities in Africa by providing legal education, training, and practice [3][4]. The organization operates with a mission to equip prisoners and prison staff with the skills to provide legal services to those who cannot afford representation [5].

impact_evidence.why_evidence_matters: Evidence ensures that legal training actually translates into freedom and fair treatment for the incarcerated.

strengths.detail: The organization maintains a <cite id="1">91/100 overall score from Charity Navigator</cite>, driven by strong governance and transparency practices.
Justice Defenders operates with high efficiency, spending only <cite id="1">$0.12 to raise every $1</cite> in contributions.
The charity holds <cite id="1">9.2 months of working capital</cite>, providing a significant buffer for long-term program stability.

strengths_deep_dive: Justice Defenders demonstrates a profound impact through its capacity-building model, which has led to the <cite id="3">release of over 15,000 individuals</cite> through legal support. Beyond direct litigation, their educational programs are highly effective, with <cite id="5">370 paralegals trained</cite> and <cite id="3">2,032 individuals released through Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR)</cite>. This dual approach of education and practice ensures that the impact is sustainable within the African justice systems they serve.
rich_narrative.summary: Justice Defenders is a specialized legal advocacy organization that empowers incarcerated individuals and prison staff in Africa through <cite id="3">legal education and professional training</cite>. founded in 2007, the organization addresses the systemic lack of legal representation by transforming prisoners into paralegals and lawyers who can provide <cite id="5">legal services to those in conflict with the law</cite>. In 2023, the organization reported <cite id="2">total revenue of $1,868,837</cite>, maintaining a stable financial base to support its operations in <cite id="3">Kenya, Uganda, and Gambia</cite>. Their unique model focuses on <cite id="3">strategic litigation and paralegal training</cite> to ensure fair hearings for defenseless communities. The organization has achieved significant scale, facilitating the <cite id="3">release of 15,338 prisoners</cite> as reported in their most recent annual cycle. By providing access to the University of London's law degree program within prison walls, they have successfully seen <cite id="3">47 law degree graduates</cite> emerge from their programs. Despite its relatively small financial footprint, the organization maintains <cite id="1">a 91/100 overall score from Charity Navigator</cite>, reflecting a high standard of institutional accountability and transparency.
